coredata.dc:title: Enhanced the zinc ion storage performance of PANI in ZnSO4 aqueous electrolyte by doping with sulfonated graphene quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Ye, Sidi
Sheng, Siqi
Liu, Yanting
Li, Yanting
Wang, Yanzhu
Yao, Hua
Meng, Lili
Chen, Qian
coredata.dc:description: Polyaniline (PANI) is a kind of low-cost and sustainable electrode material for large-scale aqueous zinc ion batteries. However, PANI always shows poor cycling performance because of deprotonation in cheap weakly acidic electrolytes. In this work, we find that sulfonated graphene quantum dots (SGQDs) dopant can alleviate the above-mentioned problem effectively because the -SO3 −groups on the SGQDs can act as a local proton reservoir and increase the protonation of PANI. When tested as zinc ion cells in ZnSO4 aqueous electrolyte, the SGQDs doped PANI electrodes exhibit excellent rate performance (115 mA h/g at 1 A/g and 105 mA h/g at 5 A/g) and good cycling stability (retain 81% after 1500 cycles), which are much better than that of pure PANI. Furthermore, the chemical oxidation method used to prepare powdered PANI is easy to scale up. This work provides a potential strategy for enhancing the Zn-ion storage performance of PANI in cheap weakly acidic electrolytes by versatile carbon quantum dots materials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: PANI
Zinc ion batteries
Sulfonated graphene quantum dots
ZnSO4 aqueous electrolyte

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of edge-sulfonated graphene quantum dots dispersion

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Guangxi Province ( AD21075007 ), the Middle-aged and Young Teachers' Basic Ability Promotion Project of Guangxi ( 2020KY09027 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Scheme of preparing SGQDs dispersion and SGQDs doped PANI.
XRD patterns (a) and FT-IR spectrum (b) of the PANI and the SGQDs doped PANIs.
(a) Full scan XPS spectra of PANI and SGQDs/PANI; (b) XPS S2p PANI and SGQDs/PANI; (c-d) XPS S2p (d) and N1s (d) fits of the SGQDs4/PANI.
SEM images of (a) PANI, (b) SGQDs4/PANI, (c) SGQDs8/PANI and (d) SGQDs12/PANI.
TEM (a) and HRTEM (b) images of SGQDs4/PANI.

(a) Rate performances and the followed cycle performances of PANI and SGQDs doped PANIs; (b-c) GCD profiles of PANI(b) and SGQDs4/PANI (c) at different current densities (derived from the tenth cycle of each current density from the rate performance test).

(a) The curves of the initial few cycles of SGQDs4/PANI at 0.6 mV/s; (b) The CV curves of SGQDs4/PANI at different scan rates after being activated; (c) log(i) vs. log(v) plots of peaks in CV curves; (d) The pseudocapacitive and diffusion capacity contributions.
(a-e) Cycling performance of PANI and SGQDs/PANI at 5 A/g (a) and the corresponding GCD profiles of PANI (b), SGQDs4/PANI (c), SGQDs8/PANI (d) and SGQDs12/PANI (e) on 300th and 1500th cycles.
ex situ XRD (a) and XPS (b-f) spectra of the SGQDs4/PANI electrodes at discharge (I) and charge (II) states.
Proposed Zn2+ storage mechanism of the SGQDs4/PANI.

head.ce:title: Enhanced the zinc ion storage performance of PANI in ZnSO4 aqueous electrolyte by doping with sulfonated graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Coll Chem & Mat, Guangxi Key Lab Nat Polymer Chem & Phys, Nanning Normal University, Nanning, 530001, Guangxi, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangxi Key Lab Nat Polymer Chem & Phys

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Coll Chem & Mat, Guangxi Key Lab Nat Polymer Chem & Phys, Nanning Normal University, Nanning, 530001, Guangxi, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Polyaniline (PANI) is a kind of low-cost and sustainable electrode material for large-scale aqueous zinc ion batteries. However, PANI always shows poor cycling performance because of deprotonation in cheap weakly acidic electrolytes. In this work, we find that sulfonated graphene quantum dots (SGQDs) dopant can alleviate the above-mentioned problem effectively because the -SO3 −groups on the SGQDs can act as a local proton reservoir and increase the protonation of PANI. When tested as zinc ion cells in ZnSO4 aqueous electrolyte, the SGQDs doped PANI electrodes exhibit excellent rate performance (115 mA h/g at 1 A/g and 105 mA h/g at 5 A/g) and good cycling stability (retain 81% after 1500 cycles), which are much better than that of pure PANI. Furthermore, the chemical oxidation method used to prepare powdered PANI is easy to scale up. This work provides a potential strategy for enhancing the Zn-ion storage performance of PANI in cheap weakly acidic electrolytes by versatile carbon quantum dots materials.

The retention rate of the PANI after 1500 cycles at 5A/g increases from 43% to 81% after doping with sulfonated graphene quantum dots.
Image, graphical abstract

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Energy crisis and environmental pollution urge scientists all over the world to explore the utilization of sustainable energy, such as solar and wind power. However, the spatiotemporal distribution of these resources is disproportion, which lowers their utilization efficiency. To overcome these shortcomings, low-cost and safe energy storage systems are needed. As we know, Lithium-ion batteries are the most popular energy storage devices in portable electronic equipment and electric vehicle because of their high energy densities [1,2]. However, the high cost limits their use in large scale grid-level energy storage. Hence, many scientists are turning their eyes to aqueous electrolyte batteries which have the advantages of low cost and safety [3,4]. Among them, aqueous zinc ion battery attracts increasing attentions because the negative electrode (Zinc metal) has the characteristics of high theoretical capacity, low redox potential, and excellent compatibility with aqueous electrolytes [5–7]. In recent years, lots of inorganic materials such as Manganese/Vanadium base oxide and hexacyanoferrate are studied as positive electrodes of zinc ion batteries [8–11]. However, from the perspective of sustainable development, organic electrode materials are more suitable [12,13]. But most of the electroactive organic molecules have low conductivity and show relatively high solubility during charging/discharging in the aqueous electrolyte [14,15]. Conducting polymers with merits of high electrical conductivities and negligible solubility in aqueous electrolytes provide a feasible solution to the above problems of organic electrode materials [16]. Polyaniline (PANI), a typical conducting polymer, has been investigated as electrode materials for supercapacitors [17], Al3+/Zn2+/NH4 + batteries [18–25], and so on. However, PANI always shows poor cycling performance when used as positive electrode material of aqueous zinc ion batteries in electrolyte with low acidity such as ZnSO4 or Zn(CF3SO3)2 electrolyte which may cause the deprotonation of PANI [26–28]. In recent years, some researches have reported that building of a local proton reservoir/ environment could alleviate this problem and enhance the efficiency of the PANI during electric procedures [29–31]. For instance, graphene oxides were used as local proton reservoir to increase protonation of PANI [29]; -SO3 − self-dopant was introduced as an internal proton reservoir to ensure locally high acidic environment and facilitate the redox process in the weak acidic ZnSO4 electrolyte [32], and N-doped carbon framework with rich pyrrolic-N groups was used for coordinator to regulate the local protonation environment of PANI to promote the redox reactions [33]. However, some of these works involve complex prepared processes or consuming large amounts of expensive reagents which limit their large-scale synthesis and application. Therefore, exploration of a strategy having the advantages of low cost and easy realization of large-scale production is very essential.

Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs), a kind of highly tunable and versatile zero-dimensional carbon-based material, have been studied in many fields such as ion sensing, bioimaging optoelectronics, and electronic devices after being modified/doped by different components [34]. In recent years, GQDs are intensively investigated in electrochemical energy storage devices [34–38]. For example, researchers have found that amine-functionalized single-crystalline graphene quantum dots showed excellent capacitive performance [39], the composite consisted of ferrocenyl modified graphene quantum dot and polypyrrole delivered high capacitance and excellent cycling performance [40], and GQD could enhance the capacitive performance of the ternary polymer nanocomposite (PVB, PANI and PEDOT: PSS) significantly [41].

Inspired by the above strategies, in this work, we select edge-sulfonated graphene quantum dots (SGQDs) as dopants, and a facile chemical oxidation method is used to prepare PANI powder. The PANI doped with proper content of SGQDs can deliver a higher reversible discharge capacity and better rate performance than that of pure PANI. Especially, the suitable content of SGQDs can enhance the long-term cycling stability of the PANI in cheap aqueous ZnSO4 electrolyte obviously. The phenomenon of enhanced cycling performance in low-cost weak acidic electrolyte may be attributed to the proton doping effect of -SO3 − in SGQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of edge-sulfonated graphene quantum dots dispersion
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of sulfonated graphene quantum dots is similar to the previous work [42]. Pyrene (1.5 g, MACKLIN, purity 97%) was refluxed and stirred for 12 h in HNO3 (120 ml) at 80 °C to nitrate into 1, 3, 6-trinitropyrene. After cooling to room temperature, the resulting precipitates were vacuum-filtered and washed with deionized water and dried at 60 °C overnight in an oven. The resultant yellow 1,3,6-trinitropyrene (0.73 g) was dispersed in 50 ml of Na2SO3 solution (0.5 M). The suspension was transferred to a 100 ml poly(tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 12 h. After cooling to room temperature, SGQDs dispersion was obtained.

The SGQDs/PANI composite was synthesized using in-situ chemical oxidative polymerization. First, 1.8 ml aniline monomer was dispersed into 75 ml 1 M HCl solution, and the system was stirred for 10 min in an ice bath. Then, the required amount of SGQDs dispersion (5 ml, 10 ml, and 15 ml) was added and stirred for 10 min. 1.14 g ammonium persulfate (APS) dissolved in 25 ml HCl solution (1 M) was added into above solution slowly. When the reaction continued for 2 h, the beaker was put in the fridge for 12 h. The samples were obtained by vacuum-filtered and washed with deionized water and ethanol in order, and then dried at 60 °C overnight in an oven. The SGQDs contents of the resulting samples are about 4% (labeled as SGQDs4/PANI), 8% (SGQDs8/PANI) and 12% (SGQDs12/PANI), respectively. For comparison, the pure PANI was synthesized without SGQDs. And other control experiments with the addition of Na2SO3, NaNO2 and/or NaNO3 during the synthetic process were also conducted.

The morphologies of the as-prepared samples were characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, TESCAN MIRA LMS) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Talos F200S G2). Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR) was performed on Thermo Scientific iN10. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was measured on an XPS spectrometer (Thermo Scientific K-Alpha) with Al-Kα radiation as the excitation source. X-ray diffraction (XRD) was recorded on a X'Pert PRO with Cu-Kα radiation. N2 isothermal adsorption-desorption measurements were performed on Micromeritics APSP 2460 to evaluate the surface area and pore-size distribution of the composites. The particle size distributions of the samples were measured by laser particle analyzer (Malvern Mastersizer 3000).

CR2032-type coin cells were assembled to evaluate the electrochemical performance. The positive electrodes are prepared by mixing the PANI or SGQDs/PANI, Acetylene black and poly(vinylidene fluoride) (PVDF) with a weight ratio of 7:2:1 in 1-Methyl-2-pyrrolidinone (NMP) and stirring. The resulting slurry is coated onto carbon paper (diameter:15 mm) and dried at 60 °C for 24 h. The weight of the electrodes was recorded on the LICHEN Electronic Balance with the sensitivity of 0.01 mg. The weight difference between the resulting electrode and the carbon paper is multiplied by 0.7 to obtain the mass of the active material, which is 2.5–3.0 mg. The PANI or SGQDs/PANI electrode, glass microfiber filters with a diameter of 16 mm (Whatman, GF/A), Zn foils (thickness: 100 µm) and 1.0 M ZnSO4 aqueous solution were served as the positive electrodes, separators, negative electrodes, and electrolyte, respectively. The galvanostatic charge/discharge tests were implemented in a voltage range of 0.5 - 1.6 V by using the Neware battery test system (CT-4008Tn-5V50mA-HWX). Cyclic voltammetry (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) tests were carried out on an electrochemical workstation (CHI660E). In order to accurately compare the material performance in batteries, all the specific capacities and current densities are based on the weight of active materials.

The synthesis procedure of SGQDs dispersion and SGQDs/PANI is showed in Fig. 1 . As displayed, the SGQDs dispersion is obtained by facile hydrothermal method and SGQDs doped PANI (or PANI) is prepared by traditional chemical oxidation method. These processes are easy to scale up.

The structural information of PANI and the SGQDs doped PANIs (SGQDs/PANI) are characterized by XRD and FTIR. In the XRD pattern of PANI (Fig. 2 a), three broad diffraction peaks appear near 15.2°, 20.1° and 25.4°, suggesting the half-doped state form of PANI [43]. Meanwhile, SGQDs/PANI exhibits similar peaks with pure PANI, and no additional diffraction peaks are observed, which indicates that the samples are relatively pure.

Fig. 2b shows the FTIR spectra of PANI, SGQDs4/PANI, SGQDs8/PANI, and SGQDs12/PANI composites. In the spectrum of PANI, strong peak is found at 3445 cm−1, which corresponds to N-H stretching [44]. Moreover, the peaks at 1567 cm−1 and 1476 cm−1 correspond to C=C stretching vibration of quinoid rings and benzenoid, respectively. The peaks at 1297 and 1240 cm−1 are assigned to the C=N and C–N stretching modes of the secondary aromatic amines. The characteristic absorption peaks at 1111 and 875 cm−1 are caused by the C–H stretching vibration in quinoid and benzenoid rings, while the peak observed at 791 cm−1 corresponding to the C–H stretching vibration of the 1, 4-disubstituted benzene's ring out of plane deformation vibration [20,45-47]. By comparing, on the FTIR spectra of SGQDs doped PANI samples, several additional peaks can be observed. Among them, the new peak at ∼1027 cm−1 is ascribed to stretching vibration of -SO3 − [42,48,49], which demonstrates the successful introduction of SGQDs into polyaniline. It should be noted that the spectra of all samples display bands near 591, 618 and 882 cm−1 which can be alloted to S-OH or S-O-C stretching and S-O bending modes. The appearance of HSO4 −/HSO3 − or SO4 2− in pure PANI is attributed to the use of APS as oxidant during synthetic process and they can act as additional dopants [50,51].

Elemental analysis of the composites was carried out using the X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS). As displayed in Fig. 3 a, the survey spectrum of PANI shows five obvious signals, corresponding to the S 2p, Cl 2p, C 1 s, N 1 s and O 1 s peaks, respectively. The binding energies located at ∼285.1 and ∼399.1 eV represent C 1 s and N 1 s respectively, in line with the reported value of PANI [52]. The binding energy located at ∼532.1 eV belongs to the O 1 s peak, generated by the APS byproduct. Excluding the peaks, the signals of Cl (∼197.1 eV) can be also observed which reveals that PANI exists in a doped state. The S 2p peak (∼168.1 eV) appears on PANI spectrum can be associated with the use of APS as oxidant and they can act as additional dopants [50]. When doped with SGQDs, the XPS spectra of the as-prepared samples are almost the same as the PANI. However, as the amount of SGQDs increasing, the intensity of S 2p peak increases (Fig. 3b), while that of the Cl 2p peak decreases gradually and disappears when the content of SGQDs reaches ∼12% (Fig. S1), demonstrating that SGQDs have been doped into PANI successfully [53]. The atomic ratios of the C, N, S and Cl given in the Table S1 further prove the above-mentioned conclusions. The S 2p high-resolution spectrum of SGQDs4/PANI (Fig. 3c) is fitted using two doublets that reflect the S 2p spin-orbit interaction. The doublet with lower binding energy (BE) belongs to the sulfur of the sulfonate or sulfate group that protonates the amine ring units or the imine ring units, leading to cationic nitrogen sites. And the doublet with the higher BE is assigned to the sulfur in neutral sulfonic or sulfate acid [50,54]. The percentages of sulfonate and sulfate groups are calculated to be 0.73, 0.77, 0.79, and 0.84 for PANI, SGQDs4/PANI, SGQDs8/PANI, and SGQDs12/PANI respectively (Table S2). The XPS N 1 s fits of the SGQDs4/PANI are shown in Fig. 3d. As displayed, four peaks located near 398.5, 399.3, 400.4 and 401.9 eV are related to non-protonated imine -N=, non-protonated amine -NH-, protonated imine -NH+- and protonated amine –NH+= [33,47,55,56], respectively. The percentages of the N sites and doping level (N +/N) of the as-prepared samples are listed in Table S3. Notably, the percentages of the positively charged nitrogen in the samples increase with the increasing of SGQDs, demonstrating that greater doping levels in PANI are achieved when combined with SGQDs.

SEM images of the PANI, SGQDs4/PANI, SGQDs8/PANI and SGQDs12/PANI are displayed in Fig. 4 . As shown in Fig. 4a, the pure PANI sample shows polyaniline-specific coral-like structure co-exist with some PANI fibrils, which is similar to that reported in a previous work [46]. In contrast, while SGQDs are added, the primary PANI particles aggregate into secondary PANI particles (Fig. 4b-d) more obviously. And when the addition of SGQDs dispersion is 5 ml, there exist lots of fiber-like structures to connect the secondary particles together in the prepared composite (SGQDs4/PANI in Fig. 4b). Interestingly, with the amount of SGQDs continuously increased, the fiber-like structures turn shorter (SGQDs8/PANI in Fig. 4c) and finally disappear when the amount of SGQDs is increased to 15 ml. This could be attributed to the increase of SGQDs content, the SGQDs offer more reactive sites (-SO3 − on the SGQDs) to combine with the aniline salt by electrostatic interaction and there has no sufficient aniline salt to form PANI fibrils [57,58]. This phenomenon is similar to that of the previously reported works [57,59].

To further confirm the introduction of SGQDs in sGQDs4/PANI, TEM technic is conducted. As shown in Fig. 5 a and b, the images clearly show that the SGQDs are spherical (red circle). We can see in our work that except for the dispersed quantum dots, some of the SGQDs are connected in a line (red arrow), which is consistent with the fiber-like structure found in the SEM images. The SEM and TEM results demonstrate that SGQDs can regulate the PANI structure, which may be caused by the interactions between -SO3 − groups and PANI backbones. The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image (Fig. 5b) shows a well-defined crystalline structure with the lattice fringe distance of ∼0.23 nm, which is corresponding to the (100) diffraction plane of graphitic structure. This result is similar to that of the previous reports on SCQDs [60–63].

The particles size distributions of the samples are analyzed with laser particle size analyzer. As displayed in Fig. S2, the particle size of PANI is widely distributed. After addition of SGQDs, the distributions of the particle size are relatively concentrated. The average diameters (d50) of PANI, SGQDs4/PANI, SGQDs8/PANI and SGQDs12/PANI are calculated to be 62.6, 16.8, 29.9 and 20.8 μm, respectively. The significant shrinkages of particle size after doping with SGQDs can be ascribed to the electrostatic interaction between the aniline salt and the SGQDs during the polymerization process mentioned above. Among the as-prepared samples, SGQDs4/PANI has the smallest average particle size. Small particle size facilitates the diffusion of electrolyte ions to the internal surface of the porous particles through the channels, bringing about good rate performance. It should be noted that the diameters of the samples are larger than that shown in SEM images, this could be attributed to the aggregation of the samples which makes them difficult to disperse during the pre-processing process.

The N2 adsorption-desorption technology is conducted to estimate the pore size distribution and specific surface area of PANI and SGQDs/PANI composites. As shown in Fig. S3, The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area of PANI, SGQDs4/PANI, SGQDs8/PANI, and SGQDs12/PANI are calculated to be 43, 47, 31, and 43 m2/g, respectively. According to the DFT graphs (inset in Fig. S3), all materials have pores with widths between 2 and 150 nm, with the average pore size in PANI, SGQDs4/PANI, SGQDs8/PANI, and SGQDs12/PANI being 23, 17, 20, and 19 nm, respectively. Interestingly, for the samples doped with SGQDs, bimodal pore size distributions are observed, which could be attributed to the interactions between SGQDs and PANI chains. The interactions result in changes of morphologies and pore size distributions.

To investigate the zinc ion storage performance of as prepared samples, aqueous zinc ion batteries are assembled and tested. PANI or SGQDs/PANI, glass fiber membrane, 1.0 M ZnSO4 aqueous solution and Zn foils (thickness: 100 µm) are used as positive electrodes, separators, electrolyte and negative electrodes, respectively. As shown in Fig. S4 and Fig. 6 a, the SGQDs4/PANI electrode tested in cut-off voltage range of 0.5–1.7 V shows relatively poor stability when compared with that of tested in different cut-off voltage ranges of 0.5–1.5 V and 0.5–1.6 V. It is reported that PANI is prone to degradation in full oxidization form [32]. Therefore, the cut-off voltage range of the assembled battery in this work is ranged from 0.5 to 1.6 V. For the sake of clarity, Fig. 6a also shows the rate performances and the followed cycling performances of PANI, SGQDs8/PANI and SGQDs12/PANI. As shown, the SGQDs doped PANI samples have better rate performance than that of PANI. When the content of SGQDs is about 4%, the as-prepared sample delivers the highest specific capacity of 115 mA h/g at 1 A/g, which is comparative or even better than that of some reported organic electrode materials (Table S4 in the Supplementary Material). But with the SGQDs increased continually, the specific capacities of SGQDs/PANI begin to decline. At the same time, the cycling performances of SGQDs4/PANI and SGQDs8/PANI are much better than that of PANI and SGQDs12/PANI. Therefore, the proportion of SGQDs in composite is controlled at 4% in the following section. Fig. 6b-c exhibit the corresponding charge–discharge curves of PANI (Fig. 6b) and SGQDs4/PANI (Fig. 6c) at different current densities. As illustrated, with the current density increased, the distances of the plateaus on the charge-discharge profiles of PANI increase more significantly than that of SGQDs4/PANI, further indicating that the latter has better rate performance.

The CV curves of the initial few cycles of the as-prepared SGQDs4/PANI recorded at 0.6 mV/s are listed in Fig. 7 a. It can be seen that after 5 cycles, the CV curves tended to overlap, indicating that the electrochemical performance of the electrode tends to be stable. The CV curves recorded at different scan rates after being activated 15 cycles are also displayed in Fig. 7b. As illustrated, with continuously increased scan rates, the current intensity of the redox peaks is also enhanced. However, the potentials of the peaks shift slightly and the shapes of the curves are retained well, indicating that the electrode can withstand a fast discharging/charging kinetics process [64]. The relationship between peak current and scan rate is given by equation i= avb , where v and i are the scan rate and corresponding peak current respectively, while a and b are adjustable parameters. If the b value is close to 0.5, the capacity is controlled by faradaic intercalation process and if the value of b approaches 1, the capacity is dominated by surface-control [20,65]. From the transformation of this equation, we could see that the log(i) and log(v) show linear relationship, and the slope of the fitted line is b value. The plots of log(i) vs. log(v) according to the CV curves in Fig. 7b are fitted in Fig. 7c. As shown, the b values of peak1 and peak2 in Fig. 7b are 0.83 and 0.73 respectively. These high b values display that surface-controlled behaviors dominate the charge/discharge process of the battery, resulting in fast kinetics [20]. Furthermore, the percentages of surface-controlled capacity contribution are calculated to be 56%, 58%, 59%, 61% and 62% at scan rates of 0.6, 0.7, 0.8, 0.9 and 1 mV/s via equation i = k 1 v + k 2 v 1/2 (Fig. 7d), respectively. Such high capacitive contributions result in the fast kinetics and excellent rate performance of the battery. The EIS plots of the SGQDs/PANI//Zn and PANI//Zn batteries are displayed in Fig. S5. It is found that in the high-frequency region, the diameters of the semicircles on the EIS plots of SGQDs/PANI//Zn are smaller than that of PANI//Zn, which refer to lower charge transfer resistances [33]. Besides, the slope of the SGQDs4/PANI//Zn in low-frequency areas is greater than that of other devices, suggesting the fastest ion diffusion rate [24,30]. Moreover, the real part of impedance (Z’) as a function of low frequencies (ω) at low frequencies for all samples is also displayed in Fig. S6. The smallest slope of linear relationship for SGQDs4/PANI//Zn indicates the fastest ionic diffusion in the electrochemical processes [24,66,67].

To further evaluate the cycling performance of PANI and SGQDs doped PANI, long-term GCD tests of the as-prepare samples are further conducted. As displayed in Fig. 8 a, the specific capacities of all electrodes undergo an increase during initial cycles, which may be attributed to the broad distribution of oxidation states in the as-prepared PANI, and full electrochemical activity could be released after sufficient reduction during early cycles [32,68]. The PANI electrode shows fast capacity decay after activating and retains only 43% of the highest specific capacity. After doping with 4% of SGQDs, the cycling stability of the PANI (SGQDs4/PANI) is improved significantly and the coulombic efficiency is approximately 100% during cycling (Fig. S7), which is better than lots of reported organic electrodes (Table S4). When the content of SGQDs increases to 8%, the electrode (SGQDs8/PANI) shows excellent capacity retention rate (91%) like SGQDs4/PANI but with lower capacity. However, when the amount of SGQDs continuously increases to 12%, the electrode shows the fastest capacity decay and has a retention rate of only 16% after 1500 cycles. This phenomenon may be attributed to the continuous increase of SGQDs content which could cause too tightly connections between the polyaniline chains and be bad for buffering the expansion and shrinkage of PANI during repeated charge and discharge. The corresponding charge/discharge profiles of the 300th and 1500th cycles are also listed in Fig. 8(b-e). By comparison, we can see that the 300th GCD profiles of the SGQDs doped samples are almost identical, implying the Zn2+ storage mechanisms of the SGQDs doped samples are similar. Furthermore, the plateaus of the SGQDs4/PANI and SGQDs8/PANI are not change after 1500 cycles long-term repeated charge and discharge, further demonstrating their excellent cycling stabilities. The cycling performances of the PANI prepared with additions of different sodium salts (NaNO2, NaNO3 and/or Na2SO3) are also tested and displayed in Fig. S8-S9. As shown, the cycling stabilities of the PANI with the additions of different sodium salts are similar to that of the PANI synthesized only at the present of HCl and APS, demonstrating that the sodium salts in the SGQDs dispersion have no effect on the cycling performances of the prepared samples.

To investigate the Zn2+ storage mechanism, ex situ XRD and XPS spectra of the SGQDs4/PANI electrodes at discharge (I) and charge (II) states (the 30th cycle) are observed. As displayed in the selected ex situ XRD patterns in Fig. 9 a, there has a characteristic peak of Zn4SO4(OH)6·5H2O (PDF#39–0688) diffraction peak near 8° on the XRD patterns of the discharge state, demonstrating the formation of Zn4SO4(OH)6·5H2O during discharge process. This is caused by the increase of OH− concentration near the surface of the electrode materials and suggests a proton involved energy storage process [32]. When the electrode is charged, H + ions are deintercalated from the SGQDs4/PANI along with the dissolution of Zn4SO4(OH)6·5H2O. Fig. 9b shows the Zn 2p XPS spectrum of the SGQDs4/PANI electrode in the discharged (I) and charged (II) states. At the fully discharged state, the SGQDs4/PANI electrode shows strong Zn 2p3/2 signal, this could be attributed to three possible reasons [33,64]: the formation of Zn4SO4(OH)6⋅5H2O, the Zn2+ insertion process and zinc salt absorbed on the surface of the electrode. It is reported that the two components correspond to the octahedral (near 1022.7 eV) and tetrahedral (near 1023.3 eV) Zn in the structure of Zn4SO4(OH)6·5H2O are fitted with 3:1 ratio [32], respectively. However, in our work, the area of the peak near 1023.3 eV is much larger than that of the peak near 1022.7 eV. The enhanced intensity near 1023.3 eV may come from the Zn2+ insertion involved in the GCD process [33,64]. When the electrode is charged to 1.6 V, there is only one component (near 1022.8 eV) of Zn 2p3/2, which can be attributed to some residual (zinc salt) surface absorption [64]. The high-resolution N 1 s XPS spectra (Fig. 9c and Fig. 9d) show that there are three N sites of -NH-, -NH+-, and -N −- in the discharged electrode. The reduced -N −- species may be derived from the binding of Zn2+ to -N= [20,33]. When charged to 1.6 V, the peak of -N −- fades away and an extra oxidized site of -NH+= appears in the electrode. The S 2p peaks of the electrodes in fully charge and discharge states are also fitted. As shown, the ratio of neutral sulfonic (the doublet with higher binding energy) / sulfonate or sulfate group (the doublet with lower binding energy) is increased as the electrode is discharged from 1.6 V to 0.5 V, attributing to the formation of Zn4SO4(OH)6⋅5H2O [32]. Furthermore, the XPS signal of Cl element after 30 cycles (Fig. S10) disappeared when compared with the raw SGQDs4/PANI (Fig. S1), which demonstrates that the doped Cl− ions can be replaced by SO4 2− ions gradually during repeated charging and discharging.

From the analysis of the above results, it can be proposed that when the battery is discharged, the oxidized components on the PANI chains are converted into -NH- and -N −- species along with the co-intercalation of Zn2+ and H + ions [33]. Afterwards, when the battery is charged, the SGQDs4/PANI electrode goes through the reverse process (Fig. 10 ). Therefore, the good zinc ion storage performance of SGQDs4/PANI in ZnSO4 weak acidic electrolyte may be attributed to the following factors [29,32,33]: firstly, the -SO3 − in SGQDs is similar to the reported -SO3 − self-dopant or graphene oxide dopant in the PANI backbone, which could function as local proton reservoirs to ensure high local H + concentration and make the redox process of PANI easier to carry out in the weakly acidic electrolyte; Secondly, the highly reversible Zn2+/H + ion intercalation/ deintercalation mechanism is good for the rapid redox reaction kinetics of the SGQDs4/PANI electrode.

In summary, we have reasonably designed and synthesized PANI powder materials for zinc ion batteries in low-cost ZnSO4 aqueous electrolyte by using SGQDs as dopant. Benefitting from the local proton reservoir function of the SGQDs, the zinc ion battery using SGQDs4/PANI as positive electrode tested in ZnSO4 aqueous electrolyte delivers an excellent rate performance (105 mA h/g at 5 A/g) and a good capacity retention rate of 81% after 1500 cycles at 5 A/g, which is much better than that of pure PANI. Besides, the chemical oxidation method for preparing SGQDs doped PANI is easy to scale up. Therefore, the locally protonated PANI fabricated by doping with sulfonated graphene quantum dots could provide a potential strategy to enhance the electrochemical performance of PANI in low-cost weakly acid aqueous electrolyte.

Sidi Ye: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Siqi Sheng: Conceptualization, Investigation. Yanting Liu: Data curation. Yanting Li: . Yanzhu Wang: Investigation. Hua Yao: Methodology, Data curation, Writing – review & editing. Lili Meng: Project administration. Qian Chen: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Guangxi Province (AD21075007), the Middle-aged and Young Teachers' Basic Ability Promotion Project of Guangxi (2020KY09027).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.electacta.2023.143396.
